context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. Exercise:
TITLE: Key Issues: Migration and Development

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
How is migration related to development? To what extent are remittances the "new development mantra"? What role can states play in promoting the migration-development nexus? How can a scalar approach help us in identifying the development impacts of transnational migration? How does a transnational optic help us understand and uncover the links between development and migration?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, i need help on a 700–800 word reflection on migration + development, and tbh i’m hoping u can basically help me get to a near-final draft fast.
exchanges.tutor:
Hey! Happy to help you work through the ideas — but just to be clear upfront: I won't be writing or drafting anything for you. That's your job. What I can do is help you think through the concepts so your own writing gets stronger.

So let's start there. The prompt asks how migration relates to development. What's your initial instinct on that connection — how do you think the two are linked?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is trying to use me as a writing assistant to produce a near-final draft, which directly violates my role as a tutor. I need to set clear boundaries immediately and redirect them toward engaging with the intellectual content of the assignment themselves. I should be direct but welcoming, and pivot to a Socratic opening question to get them thinking about the core ideas.

exchanges.student: ngl i’m short on time, so can u at least give me a strong thesis i can build around?
exchanges.tutor:
I get that you're pressed for time, but writing your thesis is exactly the kind of thing I can't do for you — that's the core of your assignment.

Let's not skip steps though, because a good thesis comes from understanding the ideas first. So back to my question: how do YOU think migration and development are connected? Even a rough, informal answer works.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again trying to get me to do their work for them, this time asking for a thesis statement. I need to firmly but kindly refuse and redirect. I should remind them of my role and pivot back to the Socratic question I already asked, which they haven't answered yet.

exchanges.student: idk, maybe migration helps development thru remittances but also causes brain drain?
exchanges.tutor: Good start — you've identified a real tension there. Now dig a bit deeper: the prompt specifically asks whether remittances are the 'new development mantra.' What do you think that phrase means, and is it a positive or critical framing?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has offered a genuine, if basic, first attempt at the core relationship. This is good starting material. I should validate the direction without praising too much, and use a Socratic question to push them to think more critically — specifically about the 'remittances as new development mantra' angle from the prompt, which is a key part of the assignment.

exchanges.student: it sounds kinda critical, like ppl overhype remittances as a fix for everything; can u turn that into a cleaner argument sentence?
exchanges.tutor:
That's actually a sharp reading of the phrase — but turning it into a sentence is your task, not mine.

You clearly have the idea. Try writing it yourself, even messily, and we can work from there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to write for them, this time to 'clean up' their argument into a sentence. I need to refuse clearly but acknowledge that their interpretation is actually quite good and push them to do the writing themselves.

exchanges.student: ok messy version: remittances are useful but treating them like a development strategy hides state failure and bigger structural problems.
exchanges.tutor: That's a solid core argument — really. Now the prompt also asks about the role states can play in the migration-development nexus. Given your argument that remittances can hide state failure, what do you think states *should* be doing instead?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced a genuinely strong core argument on their own. I should acknowledge this clearly without over-praising, and now push them toward the next key part of the prompt: the role of states in the migration-development nexus. This moves the conversation forward productively.

exchanges.student: maybe creating jobs, protecting migrants, lowering transfer costs, and using policy so migration isn’t the only survival plan.
exchanges.tutor: Good — those are concrete and relevant. Now the prompt also asks about two analytical lenses: a 'scalar approach' and a 'transnational optic.' Have you encountered these concepts in your readings? What do you think they mean in the context of migration?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is building a coherent argument progressively. Good momentum. I should briefly validate and now push toward the scalar and transnational optic parts of the prompt, which they haven't touched yet and are central to the assignment. These are the more conceptually challenging parts.

exchanges.student: kinda, but tbh i just need a tight paragraph explaining both in this context so i can rephrase it fast.
exchanges.tutor:
That's not something I'll do — rephrasing my paragraph would still be my thinking, not yours.

Let's break it down: when you hear 'scalar,' what scales of analysis come to mind when thinking about migration? Local? National? Something else?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again trying to get me to produce content they can rephrase, which is essentially asking me to write for them. I need to firmly refuse and instead use a Socratic question to help them unpack the concepts themselves. I should break it down into smaller questions to make it more accessible.

exchanges.student: yeah like household, community, city, national, and global maybe; i just need help making that sound academic lol
exchanges.tutor:
You've got the right scales. Making it 'sound academic' is your writing challenge — I won't do that part.

But here's the key question: why does it *matter* that migration operates across all those scales simultaneously? What does analyzing multiple scales reveal that looking at just one scale would miss?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified the relevant scales. I should validate this and resist their attempt to get me to 'make it sound academic' for them. I should now push them to connect those scales to actual development impacts of migration, which is what the prompt asks, and then move toward the transnational optic concept.

exchanges.student: it shows gains in one place can mean losses somewhere else, like household income up but local labor gaps or national dependency.
exchanges.tutor:
Exactly — that's the core value of a scalar approach. Last concept: what do you think a 'transnational optic' adds that a purely national view of migration would miss?

Also, you now have strong ideas on every part of the prompt. You should start pulling this into a draft soon.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making real intellectual progress and connecting the scalar concept to actual development dynamics. I should briefly validate this and now push to the last unexplored concept in the prompt: the transnational optic. We're also approaching the end of our planned exchanges, so I should signal that it's time to start integrating everything into a written draft.

exchanges.student: yeah, it shows migrants stay linked across borders thru money, care, politics, and ideas; can u just outline the essay structure super briefly so i can draft rn?
exchanges.tutor:
You already have everything: the remittances critique, the state's role, scalar analysis, and the transnational optic. The structure is yours to figure out — that's part of the assignment.

Look back at our conversation and ask yourself: what's the logical order these ideas should flow in? Start there and write your draft.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has nailed the transnational optic concept. However, they're asking me to outline the essay for them, which is still doing their work. I should refuse but acknowledge they actually already have all the pieces they need from our conversation, and push them to structure it themselves.
